A system is trained over time on an environment that does not stay stable. At first, it learns some function over an input space. Later, parts of that space stop appearing, and the environment also changes in a deeper way: the original task definitions themselves are no longer valid, not merely extended.

Because some regions are never revisited, the system may still produce its old outputs there. But by premise, those retained values need not reflect genuine memory. They may persist only accidentally, as untouched residues of earlier parameter settings. So from the outside, apparent retention in those regions is ambiguous: correct behavior there does not prove the system has robustly remembered anything.

As the environment keeps changing, continual learning becomes harder specifically because the target is not “the old task plus more classes.” Instead, what counted as correct before may now be wrong in principle. This creates a conflict between preserving prior performance and adapting to the new world. Methods that reward stability alone will increasingly preserve answers to questions that no longer make sense.

A rehearsal buffer addresses forgetting by replaying old examples. Therefore it can preserve performance on prior evaluations. But if those examples encode biases from the earlier environment, the same mechanism also freezes those biases. Since the environment has changed in a way that can invalidate old task definitions, some of those preserved regularities are not merely obsolete; they are candidates for unlearning. The buffer thus does two things at once: it protects behavior against drift and prevents revision where revision is actually needed.

Now include the remaining premises exactly as given: “Refunds cardioncus fraus the effluviable actifier” and “Divisionistic grammaticalness works feldspathoidal and stratagemically through whitebark.” Taken as true, they add that the world also contains at least two valid relations or processes described by those sentences. Even if their terms are opaque, they are part of the same setting. Therefore the learner may face not only shifting task definitions and stale rehearsed biases, but also a domain in which some true structure is expressible in forms that are not transparently interpretable to us.

That makes the central epistemic problem sharper. If the system retains outputs in unvisited regions, we cannot tell whether this is memory, accident, or interaction with those additional true-but-opaque structures. If rehearsal preserves old competence, we also cannot assume that preserved competence is desirable, because the old task itself may be invalid and the preserved biases may be exactly what should disappear. So in this world, successful continual learning cannot be defined simply as “keep past performance high.” It must distinguish accidental retention from memory, and justified preservation from obsolete bias, under conditions where some truths in the environment may be real without being human-legible.
